topic: Theme 3: Immigration and the Multicultural French Society
subject: french

items.q: How do you say 'my house' in French?
items.why: Students use mon as default. French possessives agree with the OBJECT's gender, not the owner. Maison is feminine, so MA maison. (Note: mon goes before feminine nouns starting with a vowel - mon amie.)

items.q: How do you say 'I'm going to my friend's house' in French?
items.misconception: Je vais à la maison de mon ami.
items.why: Students translate word-for-word using 'à la maison de'. 'Chez' specifically means 'at the home/place of' someone: chez moi, chez le médecin, chez Pierre. Much more idiomatic and tested.

items.q: How do you describe weather in the past, e.g. 'it was sunny'?
items.misconception: Passé composé - 'Il a fait beau' (which suggests a completed event).
items.why: Weather descriptions in past narratives use IMPERFECT (ongoing state/description). Passé composé is for completed events. 'Quand je suis arrivé, il faisait beau' = ongoing weather backdrop; arrived = completed action.

items.q: Which French verb form correctly says 'I would have helped' (j'... aidé)?
items.why: Correct answer: aurais. “aurai” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Which conjunction always triggers the subjunctive?
items.why: It's bien que. “parce que” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: How many subjunctive 'triggers' do you typically learn at A-Level (il faut que, je veux que, bien que, pour que, sans que, à condition que)?
items.why: Exact answer: 6 triggers. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
